name: Brass Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Brass. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Brass oxide removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for optimal Brass absorption and minimal reflectivity

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal on Brass

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of surface contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete oxide layer removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive heat accumulation

power.description: Typical range for industrial 1064 nm laser cleaning of reflective metals like brass to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100W for light contamination. Max limited by brass's high reflectivity and melting point (~900°C). Source: CleanLaser and IPG Photonics specs for metal cleaning systems.

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds balance cleaning efficiency and heat input; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., oxides), faster for light rust/oil. Recommended 1500 mm/s for uniform brass surface prep. Source: Industry standards from Laserax and academic studies on scan optimization (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of brass; 10 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ) due to brass's thermal conductivity (~120 W/mK). Picosecond (<1 ns) for ultra-fine work but less common industrially. Source: Laser physics research in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2015) on metal ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: 20-50 kHz balances speed and heat accumulation on reflective brass; higher rates (>50 kHz) risk overheating despite good conductivity. Source: Academic literature on pulsed laser cleaning (e.g., Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019), noting trade-offs for Cu-based alloys.

energy_density.description: Fluence below brass damage threshold (~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for contaminant removal without substrate ablation; 1.5 J/cm² safe for oils/rust. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. (2017) for brass and similar alloys.

spot_size.description: 200-300 μm ideal for brass to achieve uniform cleaning on detailed surfaces; smaller for precision, larger for broad areas. Accounts for beam divergence on reflective surfaces. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning heads.

pass_count.description: 1-3 passes typical for complete removal of contaminants on brass; multi-pass for layered dirt to stay below damage threshold. Single pass sufficient for light prep. Source: Industry case studies (e.g., Laser Cleaning Technol. reports) and practical optimization.

overlap_ratio.description: 70% overlap ensures uniform coverage without excessive heat buildup on brass; higher for fine contaminants, lower for speed. Source: Process optimization studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. (2020) for scanned laser cleaning of metals.
